notes: Syria should recognize Levantine Arabic for everyday spoken compatibility. English and French remain secondary but present, especially in urban and educated contexts.

evidence.title: North Levantine Arabic [apc] primary country is Syria
evidence.note: Digital Bible Society language profile states North Levantine Arabic [apc] has Syria as its primary country and is spoken across the Levant.

evidence.title: Britannica Students: Syria
evidence.note: Britannica says Arabic is the official and most widely spoken language and that English and French are also used, especially in urban areas and among the educated.

notes: Jordan should recognize Levantine Arabic for spoken compatibility, while English is broadly useful in cities and tourism.

evidence.title: North Levantine Arabic [apc] spoken in Jordan
evidence.note: The language profile describes Levantine Arabic [apc] as spoken in Jordan as part of the Levant.

evidence.title: Visit Jordan: Just the facts
evidence.note: Jordan Tourism Board says Arabic is official and English is widely spoken, especially in cities.

notes: Palestine should treat Levantine Arabic as the practical spoken match. English is useful in many tourism and education contexts but not universal.

evidence.title: North Levantine Arabic [apc] spoken in Palestine
evidence.note: The language profile describes Levantine Arabic [apc] as spoken in Palestine.

evidence.title: Travel Palestine tourism interpretation center
evidence.note: Official tourism content notes documentary and visitor-facing services in Arabic and English.

notes: Israel should score highly for English traveler usability. Hebrew dominates, while Arabic and Levantine Arabic remain important for Arab communities and some traveler interactions.

evidence.title: Britannica: Israel
evidence.note: Britannica describes Hebrew as official and Arabic as having special status.

evidence.title: Britannica Students: Israel
evidence.note: Britannica says Israeli Arabs speak a dialect of Levantine Arabic and that English is widely spoken.

notes: The UAE should score very highly for English traveler usability. Arabic remains official, but English is deeply embedded in everyday travel and business.

evidence.title: Experience Abu Dhabi FAQs
evidence.note: Official tourism site says English is widely spoken in Abu Dhabi and that most business transactions are conducted in English.

evidence.title: Britannica: United Arab Emirates languages and religion
evidence.note: Britannica says English is widely spoken in the UAE.

notes: Qatar should score highly for English traveler usability in public-facing contexts while Arabic remains official.

evidence.title: Visit Qatar first-time visitor guide
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance says Arabic is official and English is commonly spoken at public places.

notes: Bahrain should score highly for English traveler usability, especially in tourist and commercial settings.

evidence.title: Bahrain Tourism FAQs
evidence.note: Official tourism FAQs say English is widely spoken, especially in hotels, restaurants, shops, and tourist areas.

evidence.title: About Bahrain
evidence.note: Official tourism site says Arabic is official and English is widely spoken, with bilingual signage.

notes: Kuwait should reflect strong Arabic baseline with meaningful English usability in everyday modern services and travel contexts.

evidence.title: Britannica: Kuwait
evidence.note: Britannica says Kuwaitis speak Gulf Arabic and English is the second language taught in public schools.

evidence.title: Kuwait Government Online: Kuwait Finder app
evidence.note: Official app documentation highlights Arabic and English support for public navigation services.

notes: Egypt should match Egyptian Arabic in daily speech and give meaningful credit for English and some French among educated and tourism-facing contexts.

evidence.title: Britannica: Languages of Egypt
evidence.note: Britannica explains that Egyptians speak vernacular Egyptian Arabic and that most educated Egyptians are fluent in English or French or both.

evidence.title: Egypt Ministry of Tourism hotline service
evidence.note: Official tourism hotline is available in Arabic and English for visitors.

notes: Morocco should match Moroccan Arabic and reflect the strong practical role of French and some Spanish, plus moderate English tourism usability.

evidence.title: Visit Morocco language guide
evidence.note: Official tourism guide says Morocco's official languages are Arabic and Amazigh, virtually all Moroccans understand French, and Spanish is widespread in northern and southern Morocco.

evidence.title: Britannica: Tamazight language
evidence.note: Britannica notes that French and English are also used in Morocco.

notes: Algeria should reflect Algerian Arabic in daily speech and the continued practical importance of French, while English remains lower-coverage.

evidence.title: Britannica: Languages of Algeria
evidence.note: Britannica says most Algerians speak vernacular Arabic and that French has long functioned as a lingua franca.

evidence.title: Britannica summary: Algeria
evidence.note: Britannica summary lists Arabic, Tamazight, and French in common national use.

notes: Tunisia should match Tunisian Arabic in speech and reflect the major role of French, with English useful but less universal.

evidence.title: Britannica: Languages of Tunisia
evidence.note: Britannica says Arabic is official, Tunisian Arabic is the native dialect, French plays an important role, and English also serves as a lingua franca to a lesser extent.

evidence.title: Britannica Students: Tunisia
evidence.note: Britannica says French is widely used in commerce and government.
